title: Model capability
summary: Public benchmark and model ecosystem updates continue to improve on reasoning, computer-use, and general knowledge-work tasks.
source: Stanford AI Index / ARC Prize Leaderboard / OSWorld / Alphabet Investor Relations / Our World in Data: Artificial Intelligence

title: Enterprise adoption
summary: AI is continuing to move from experimentation into real workflows and enterprise operating systems.
source: Stanford AI Index / OECD AI Policy Observatory / Our World in Data: Artificial Intelligence / IBM Investor Relations

title: Labor displacement
summary: Public labor and enterprise sources continue to show workflow substitution pressure and more explicit workforce efficiency narratives.
source: Layoffs.fyi / International Labour Organization / World Bank Open Data / National Bureau of Economic Research / Brookings Artificial Intelligence / IBM Investor Relations

title: Policy support
summary: Policy remains mixed, but public frameworks still leave room for broad AI deployment rather than fully closing the lane.
source: OECD AI Policy Observatory / EUR-Lex AI Act / Brookings Artificial Intelligence / Eurostat

title: Corporate intent
summary: Public company and research language still points toward scaling output with AI without proportional headcount growth.
source: Stanford AI Index / Brookings Artificial Intelligence / National Bureau of Economic Research / Alphabet Investor Relations

title: Institutional friction
summary: Regulation, compliance, trust, and institutional drag still keep the clock from moving as quickly as raw capability headlines suggest.
source: OECD AI Policy Observatory / EUR-Lex AI Act / International Labour Organization / Brookings Artificial Intelligence

title: First EU AI Act obligations begin applying
summary: The first operative obligations under the EU AI Act start to apply, adding compliance and governance friction to some workplace AI deployments across the European market.
source: EUR-Lex AI Act / OECD AI Policy Observatory

title: IBM says AI may replace work tied to 7,800 back-office roles
summary: Reuters reported IBM would pause hiring for back-office functions that the company believed AI and automation could absorb over time, making labor substitution pressure explicit rather than implied.
source: Reuters AI and labor reporting / Stanford AI Index
